title: Understand: Earth's place in the universe
description: Check your understanding of Earth's place in the universe in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.
topic_path: Middle school Earth and space science - NGSS
Understand: Earth's place in the universe

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why is our year approximately $365$ days long?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: This is how long it takes Earth to travel once around the sun.
This is how long it takes the sun to travel once around Earth.
This is how long it takes the universe to travel once around Earth.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What determines the length of a year on Earth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the amount of time it takes Earth to orbit the moon once
the amount of time it takes Earth to orbit the sun once
the amount of time it takes the sun to orbit Earth once
the time required for Earth to complete one spin on its axis
the time required for Earth to complete one trip around the sun
the time required for the sun to complete one trip around Earth

assessment_items.question_content:
**What causes the sun to rise every morning?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the sun revolving around Earth
Earth revolving around the sun
the sun rotating on its axis
Earth rotating on its axis

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why do we have day and night?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The sun rotates on its axis.
Earth rotates on its axis.
The sun revolves around Earth.
Earth revolves around the sun.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What causes the sun to set every night?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Earth rotating on its axis
the sun rotating on its axis
Earth revolving around the sun
the sun revolving around Earth

assessment_items.question_content:
Mars is visible in the night sky from Earth. Most of the time, Mars is seen moving in one direction across the sky. During certain periods, however, the planet seems to move backwards as it travels through the sky.

**What type of motion is this an example of?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: apparent motion
actual motion

assessment_items.question_content:
When viewed from Earth, stars seem to travel in curved paths, or trails, through the night sky. This movement can be captured in a star trail photograph, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What type of motion do star trails result from?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: Star trails in the night sky. The trails appear as curved lines revolving around a central point in the sky.

choices.content: apparent motion
actual motion

assessment_items.question_content:
Many of the planets in our solar system are visible in Earth’s night sky. Over time, these planets are seen to travel slowly eastward relative to the stars. During certain periods, however, the planets seem to reverse direction, traveling instead to the west. This change in direction is known as retrograde motion.

**What is retrograde motion an example of?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: apparent motion
actual motion

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is true about the heliocentric model?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It shows Earth as the center of the universe.
It shows the sun as the center of the universe.
It replaced the geocentric model.
It was replaced by the geocentric model.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is true about the geocentric model?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It shows the sun as the center of the universe.
It was based on the apparent motion of the sun and stars.
It was replaced by the heliocentric model.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is true about the geocentric model?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It shows Earth as the center of the universe.
It was based on the apparent motion of the sun and stars.
It replaced the heliocentric model.
